name: blender style pesto pasta
notes:
The blender version of pesto pasta. Inclusive of greens, nuts, and vegetables that don't work in the pestle version — the blender's mechanical power lets fibrous and tough ingredients break down properly.

INCLUSIVE GREENS: basil works, but so do parsley, kale, asparagus, and carrot greens — all fibrous things the pestle couldn't handle. Spinach and mixed greens also fine. Mix and match.

INCLUSIVE NUTS: almonds work here (where they don't in the pestle). Pine nuts, cashews, sunflower seeds all still fine. The blender can pulverize any of them.

VEGETABLES AND FIBER ARE WELCOME: blender pesto loses fiber compared to pestle pesto, so adding more vegetables (asparagus, kale, carrot greens, yellow onion) helps restore nutritional density and adds body to the sauce.

HEAVY CREAM IS LOAD-BEARING: the blender benefits from a liquid component to get a smooth blend. Heavy cream is the canonical choice — it lets the sauce blend smooth without over-relying on olive oil (the non-cream version ends up oil-heavy and reads greasy rather than rich). Lemon juice can stand in as the liquid component for a lighter version. Use one or the other, not just olive oil.

CANNED TOMATOES SHINE HERE: where canned tomatoes are wrong for the pestle version, they're actually structural in the blender version. The blender breaks them down completely and the concentrated tomato flavor integrates with the green pesto base. Use as a primary ingredient.

CRUNCH ADD AT THE END: slivered almonds and sunflower seeds can be folded in AFTER blending for textural contrast — restores some of the chew you lose by blending everything smooth. Toasted is better than raw here.

ONION HANDLING: yellow onion in the blender works but the raw bite can be harsh. Sauté the onion briefly before blending to soften the edge.

FAILURE MODES (learned the hard way):

- TOO MUCH PASTA WATER AT THE END. Unlike the mortar version, this base already carries a lot of liquid (cream, lemon, canned tomatoes). Adding pasta water to loosen the sauce — the standard pasta-finishing move — can instantly ruin the dish here. Use a fraction of what you'd add to a regular pasta. Better to err on the dry side and add water tablespoon by tablespoon than to drown the sauce.

- SPAGHETTI INSTEAD OF PENNE. This is a chunky, blended-and-rebuilt sauce — closer to a pasta salad than a traditional pasta-with-sauce. It needs a pasta shape that holds chunks, picks up the sauce in pockets, and doesn't get drowned. Penne (or other tubular/short shapes) is the right call. Spaghetti slides under the chunky sauce structure and doesn't carry it. The blended-sauce-on-spaghetti combination reads off — looks and tastes wrong.

- TOO MUCH PROTEIN (NUT/SEED OVERLOAD). The blender makes it tempting to keep adding nuts or seeds to thicken the sauce instead of using restraint. Past a certain point the dish swings PROTEIN SHAKE — the sauce reads gritty, dense, and one-note. Don't try to fix a thin sauce with more nuts. The liquid/texture balance is the game here, same as any pasta. If the sauce is too thin, add more canned tomato or reduce — don't add more nuts.
